graph.topic: Exploring the preparation process, presentation strategies, and expert judgment involved in delivering oral case presentations in clinical training settings.
graph.researcher_bg: An HCI researcher focused on medical education technology who seeks to understand presentation workflows and skill acquisition in structured terms but has no clinical training background.
graph.expert_bg: A senior clinician and clinical educator who has delivered and evaluated hundreds of oral case presentations, and relies on deeply internalized judgment to organize, prioritize, and adapt case narratives for different clinical audiences.

q_01__exp_L1_00.label: Expert Clinical Presenter
q_01__exp_L1_00.description: Senior clinician perspective on organizing, prioritizing, and adapting case narratives for different clinical audiences.

q_01__exp_L2_00_00.label: Cognitive Synthesis & Story Building
q_01__exp_L2_00_00.description: How raw patient data is mentally processed into a coherent clinical narrative before delivery.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Scanning for the Clinical Story
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: Skimming the chart for red flags and the diagnostic puzzle rather than reading front-to-back.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Instinctive Outlier Detection
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: Recognizing values that don't fit the patient's baseline without needing to check references.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Crafting the Summary One-Liner
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: Condensing the patient's identity and main problem into a single opening sentence.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: Ordering problems by life-threat level, but also by what the audience needs to learn.

q_01__exp_L2_00_01.label: Audience & Context Calibration
q_01__exp_L2_00_01.description: Adjusting content and delivery based on who is listening and the constraints of the clinical setting.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Tailoring to Role & Expertise
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: Shifting focus from reasoning/differential for trainees to management decisions for seniors.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Simplifying for Multidisciplinary Teams
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Adjusting jargon and context when nurses or pharmacists are present to ensure alignment.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Adapting to Venue & Time Constraints
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: Balancing brevity for workflow rounds against depth for teaching conferences.

q_01__exp_L2_00_02.label: Delivery & Defense Mechanisms
q_01__exp_L2_00_02.description: Managing the live interaction, handling uncertainty, and defending clinical decisions during Q&A.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Navigating Uncertainty & Honesty
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: Admitting gaps in knowledge while offering a reasoned hypothesis instead of guessing.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Defending Without Arguing
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: Standing firm on the plan without becoming defensive when challenged by seniors.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Real-Time Narrative Adjustment
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: Shifting emphasis or depth mid-presentation based on audience engagement cues.

analogy.target_concept: Finding a contradiction in a friend's story

structural_mapping.inputs: Data points vs. baseline expectations
structural_mapping.logic: Recognizing mismatch without external verification
structural_mapping.outputs: Identifying hidden truth rather than correcting error

analogy.explanation: The researcher treats conflicting data as noise to be fixed for consistency, but the expert treats it as signal. Like finding a contradiction in a friend's story, the mismatch isn't an error to edit out; it is the clue that reveals the true situation.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you find conflicting labs in a chart, do you smooth them out to make the narrative consistent, or do you keep the tension because that contradiction is actually the diagnosis?

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that crafting the summary one-liner requires condensing the patient's identity and main problem into a single, consistent opening sentence. This focus ensures the clinical narrative remains accurate regardless of external variables.

pivot.limitation: Staying only on the expert's concern misses how the researcher's focus on physical settings dictates preparation length and detail.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand how environmental constraints shape the preparation strategy alongside the expert's narrative goals.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the expert's consistency is vital, the researcher's concern matters because it reveals how the expert adapts delivery to fit real-world constraints.
pivot.coarse_scenario: You've highlighted how critical the one-liner's accuracy is, but could you walk me through how you might change that preparation if you were speaking in a noisy hallway versus a quiet conference room?

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the critical nature of ordering specific problems within the assessment based on life-threat and learning opportunities. This ensures the audience grasps the most vital clinical decisions first.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on problem-level ordering overlooks how the presenter decides to allocate preparation time between the history section and the assessment section overall.

pivot.coarse_scenario: That triage of specific problems is vital, and I'm also curious about the bigger picture of how you decide to weight the assessment section against the history section during your prep.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Triage of Challenge Importance
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: When they hit you with a detail, you have to instantly decide: is this a critical safety issue I need to address now, or a minor outlier I can explain away and move past?

aliases: pulls a detail out of the chart

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: That split-second panic when you lose your train of thought or logic because of a question, and you have to bridge back without stuttering or contradicting yourself.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Integrating New Constraints
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: They hand you a new result or raise a point that doesn't fit your diagnosis, and you have to instantly rewrite your logic without pausing.
aliases: reconcile that with what we've seen

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_04.label: Deciding Engagement Depth
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_04.description: You have to decide quickly whether to dig into their question or pivot back to the main thread so you don't lose the audience.
aliases: circle back to why I think

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Distinguishing Testing vs. Correcting

aliases: not even sure what they're really asking

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Admitting Ignorance Safely

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Sensing When to Stop Defending
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: Feeling that shift in the air where pushing back anymore makes you look stubborn instead of confident, so you pivot to accepting their guidance.

q_03__exp_L1_00.label: Expert Evaluator Cues
q_03__exp_L1_00.description: Senior clinician's rapid assessment of trainee competence based on opening narrative and presence.

q_03__exp_L2_00_00.label: Narrative Architecture & Clinical Logic
q_03__exp_L2_00_00.description: Evaluating how the trainee constructs the clinical story, prioritizes data, and establishes logical flow.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: The "Anchor" Statement
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: I listen for that first sentence to tell me who this person is and why they're sick. If they stop after demographics or hit an awkward silence, they're reading a template. They need to flow immediately into the story.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Early Hypothesis Planting
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: Strong presenters hint at what they think is wrong within the first minute. If they're just listing facts without a direction, like 'here's the history' without a thread, they haven't synthesized the case yet.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Signal vs. Noise Filtering
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: You can tell immediately if they're going to read every normal lab value. I'm listening for them to skip the potassium of 4.1 and jump straight to the troponin that matters. If they drown me in normals, they don't know what's important.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Chronological Coherence
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: I need to feel the timeline. If they jump from admission meds to birth history to today's vitals without a bridge, I lose the thread. Strong presentations move like a story, not a database dump.

q_03__exp_L2_00_01.label: Presenter Presence & Ownership
q_03__exp_L2_00_01.description: Observing the trainee's confidence, delivery style, and sense of responsibility for the patient care narrative.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Eye Contact & Script Dependency
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: I watch their eyes. If their head is down reading or they're acting overly stiff/formal, they don't own the case. I need to see them looking up, engaging naturally, proving they know the data.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Vocal Delivery & Composure
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: It's not just about 'ums'. If they speak too fast, sound flat, or aren't listening to themselves, they're nervous. I listen for a steady, natural rhythm and comfortable silences versus nervous noise.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Ownership & Integrity
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: I cringe when I hear 'It was decided'. I also watch if they fudge numbers when unsure. Strong trainees claim their decisions ('I recommended') and admit gaps honestly instead of bluffing.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_03.label: Recovery from Initial Stumble
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_03.description: Everyone trips on a name or date sometimes. I'm watching how they handle that first glitch. Do they apologize and spiral, or do they correct it cleanly and keep driving? That tells me how they'll handle hard questions later.

q_03__shared_root.label: Exploring the preparation process, presentation strategies, and expert judgment involved in delivering oral case presentations in clinical training settings.
q_03__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring the preparation process, presentation strategies, and expert judgment involved in delivering oral case presentations in clinical training settings.

q_03__res_L1_00.label: Evaluator Heuristics & Cognitive Processing
q_03__res_L1_00.description: Examining how experts rapidly form judgments using mental models and cognitive shortcuts during the critical opening phase, distinct from the sustained narrative stability explored in q_02.

q_03__res_L2_00_00.label: Rapid Schema Activation
q_03__res_L2_00_00.description: Investigating the specific triggers that cause an expert to categorize a trainee as 'competent' or 'novice' within the first 30 seconds.

q_03__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Linguistic Competence Triggers
q_03__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Identify specific vocabulary or phrasing patterns that instantly activate a 'competent' mental model in the evaluator's mind before clinical accuracy is verified.

q_03__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Hesitation Tolerance Threshold
q_03__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Determine the duration and frequency of pauses or filler words allowed before the evaluator's judgment shifts from 'thinking' to 'unprepared'.

q_03__res_L2_00_01.label: Attentional Weighting
q_03__res_L2_00_01.description: Understanding how experts allocate limited cognitive resources between verbal content and non-verbal delivery cues during the initial impression.

q_03__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Verbal vs. Non-Verbal Priority
q_03__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Quantify the relative weight assigned to voice confidence (pace, tone) versus data accuracy when forming the first impression of case ownership.

q_03__res_L1_01.label: Information Architecture of the Opening
q_03__res_L1_01.description: Analyzing the structural design of the trainee's initial summary statement as an information interface that sets expectations for the rest of the presentation.

q_03__res_L2_01_00.label: Summary Statement Density
q_03__res_L2_01_00.description: Defining the optimal information density required in the 'one-liner' to establish context without overwhelming the listener's working memory.

q_03__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Minimum Viable Data Elements
q_03__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Identify the critical data points (age, symptom, timeline) that must be present in the first sentence to prevent cognitive load spikes for the listener.

q_03__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Data Sequencing Preferences
q_03__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Analyze expert preferences for the order of information delivery (e.g., demographic first vs. chief complaint first) to establish narrative flow.

q_03__res_L2_01_01.label: Structural Signposting
q_03__res_L2_01_01.description: Evaluating the effectiveness of explicit organizational cues that guide the expert's expectations for the upcoming narrative structure.

q_03__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Explicit Roadmap Utility
q_03__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Evaluate whether explicit signposting phrases ('I will cover X, Y, Z') enhance perceived organization or sound robotic and rehearsed to experts.

q_03__res_L1_02.label: Perceptual Gaps & Bias
q_03__res_L1_02.description: Exploring the misalignment between trainee intent and expert perception, focusing on biases that may form before the clinical reasoning is fully demonstrated.

q_03__res_L2_02_00.label: Signal-Noise Misalignment
q_03__res_L2_02_00.description: Investigating discrepancies between the cues trainees believe signal competence and the cues experts actually use for early judgment.

q_03__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Intended vs. Received Signals
q_03__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Uncover mismatches where trainees emphasize low-value details (noise) while experts prioritize high-value structural cues (signal) in the first minute.

q_03__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: False Positive Confidence Markers
q_03__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Identify specific behaviors that project confidence but mask weak clinical reasoning, potentially leading to erroneous early positive judgments.

q_03__res_leaf_02_00_02.label: Primacy Effect on Scoring
q_03__res_leaf_02_00_02.description: Assess the strength of the 'Halo Effect' where early impressions bias the expert's evaluation of later medical content accuracy.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the importance of trainees claiming ownership of decisions and maintaining integrity by avoiding passive voice or bluffing. This focus highlights the professional stance required during clinical presentations.

pivot.research_goal: The researcher is attempting to identify the minimum viable data elements required in the opening statement to ensure clarity.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the expert values integrity, the researcher finds that structural clarity is also necessary to ensure the listener can process the information required to appreciate that integrity.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I agree that owning your decisions is crucial. I'm also trying to map out if missing key data points in the first sentence undermines that ownership by confusing the listener before they even hear your recommendation.

q_04__exp_L1_00.label: Expert Clinical Presenter
q_04__exp_L1_00.description: Senior clinician perspective on the tacit skills, judgment, and adaptive strategies required for high-impact oral case presentations.

q_04__exp_L2_00_00.label: Cognitive Curation & Narrative Logic
q_04__exp_L2_00_00.description: The internal mental work of filtering data and constructing a logical story flow that drives clinical reasoning.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Strategic Omission & Signal Detection
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: Juniors think more words equal thoroughness. Experts know dumping normal labs makes listeners tune out. You have to spot the signal in the noise and leave the boring data in the chart.
aliases: The art of leaving things out

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Causal Chaining of Findings
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: Don't just list positives like a grocery list. Connect them. Explain why the crackles matter to the fever. Show the link between the data point and your hypothesis.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Dynamic Narrative Anchor
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: Your opening one-liner isn't set in stone. If new info arrives while rounding, subtly shift how you describe the patient by the time you hit the plan. Pivot without losing control of the story.

q_04__exp_L2_00_01.label: Listener Cognitive Load & Engagement
q_04__exp_L2_00_01.description: Managing the audience's attention and ensuring they follow the clinical reasoning without getting lost.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Signposting the Logic Path
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: Tell them where you're driving before you turn. Say 'I'm focusing on the fever first.' Build a bridge between the patient's story and what the listener needs to hear right now.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Reading the Room's Energy
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Feel when they check out. If the attending looks at their phone or stops asking questions, you're losing them. Wrap it up or pivot to the bottom line immediately.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Making the Plan Inevitable
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: The plan shouldn't sound like a guess. It should sound like the only logical next step based on the story you just told. Make them feel like arriving at any other conclusion would be weird.

q_04__exp_L2_00_02.label: Professional Identity & Uncertainty
q_04__exp_L2_00_02.description: How the presenter carries themselves when dealing with gaps in knowledge or high-stakes decisions.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Classifying the Unknown
q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: There's a difference between 'I don't know the answer' and 'I don't have the data yet.' Don't confuse them. One is a knowledge gap, the other is a workup gap. Own the specific type.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Composure Under Pressure
q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: When challenged or when the story shifts, react without flinching. Your process runs in the background so you can steer the conversation without losing confidence or control.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Pacing & Vocal Control
q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: Feel the rhythm of the room. Stop talking after the big point to let it sink in. Juniors fill silence with chatter; experts use the quiet to make the attending lean in.

q_04__res_L2_00_00.description: Differentiating between rules that can be codified in software and intuition that must be developed through experience.

q_04__res_L2_00_01.description: Isolating the specific mental processes that drive the synthesis described in q_01, focusing on capacity rather than steps.

q_04__res_L1_01.label: Instructional Scaffolding & Support
q_04__res_L1_01.description: Exploring how technology can support skill acquisition without bypassing the learning process, building on the preparation context from q_01.

q_04__res_L2_01_00.description: Deciding which cognitive tasks the system should handle versus those the trainee must perform to learn.

q_04__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Establish criteria for when system assistance should be removed to force the trainee to demonstrate independent competency.

q_04__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Explore methods to represent the structural differences in how experts vs. novices organize the same patient data.
